stem: A hospital uses a CT scan screening to screen for early-stage lung cancer. The test has a sensitivity of 96% and a specificity of 96%. The prevalence of early-stage lung cancer in the patient population is estimated to be between 1.4% and 2.1%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has early-stage lung cancer? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(early-stage lung cancer | positive test) ranges from 0.254 to 0.34 as prevalence varies from 1.4% to 2.1%. Point estimate at 1.8% prevalence: 0.306.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.04.
The confidence interval [0.254, 0.34] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (96%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
